name: Hvergelmir
description: Hvergelmir, meaning 'bubbling or roaring cauldron', is a spring located in Niflheim, the primordial realm of ice and mist in Norse cosmology. It is the source of all rivers, and thus, the source of all life. From Hvergelmir flows eleven rivers known as the Élivágar, which eventually froze and formed the ice of Niflheim. The spring is also the home of the dragon Níðhöggr, who gnaws at one of the roots of Yggdrasil, the world tree, constantly threatening the stability of the cosmos. Hvergelmir is described as a place of immense power and primordial energy, a critical component in the creation and maintenance of the Norse universe. Its constant churning and bubbling represent the chaotic forces that underlie existence. The spring's location in Niflheim reinforces its association with death, the cold, and the beginning of everything. It is a place of both creation and destruction, a nexus point in the web of Norse mythology, and a constant reminder of the cyclical nature of existence. The waters flowing from it are essential for the survival of all beings, yet the dragon within poses a constant threat.
subtitle: Bubbling cauldron, source of all rivers

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Rivers
keyMyths.description: Hvergelmir is the source of the Élivágar rivers which flowed out of Niflheim and eventually froze, creating the icy landscape.

keyMyths.title: Níðhöggr's Task
keyMyths.description: The dragon Níðhöggr dwells in Hvergelmir and gnaws at the roots of Yggdrasil, threatening to bring down the world tree.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology.
